verses.text: The sons of Aaron are named — Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, Ithamar — but Nadab and Abihu died childless, so the priesthood ran through the younger two. Even priestly privilege is no shield against judgment for irreverence; the office passed to those who remained.

The sons of Aaron are named — Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, Ithamar — but Nadab and Abihu died childless, so the priesthood ran through the younger two. Even priestly privilege is no shield against judgment for irreverence; the office passed to those who remained.

The sons of Aaron are named — Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, Ithamar — but Nadab and Abihu died childless, so the priesthood ran through the younger two. Even priestly privilege is no shield against judgment for irreverence; the office passed to those who remained.

David distributes the priests by lot, sixteen chief men from Eleazar's line and eight from Ithamar's, recorded by a scribe before the king. The lot prevents rivalry and favour. God orders His ministers fairly, each receiving his place as from His hand.

David distributes the priests by lot, sixteen chief men from Eleazar's line and eight from Ithamar's, recorded by a scribe before the king. The lot prevents rivalry and favour. God orders His ministers fairly, each receiving his place as from His hand.

David distributes the priests by lot, sixteen chief men from Eleazar's line and eight from Ithamar's, recorded by a scribe before the king. The lot prevents rivalry and favour. God orders His ministers fairly, each receiving his place as from His hand.

The first courses fall by lot — Jehoiarib, Jedaiah, and on — each priestly family given its turn at the temple. Worship runs in orderly rotation. The eighth course, Abijah, would one day be Zacharias's, on duty when John's birth was announced.

The first courses fall by lot — Jehoiarib, Jedaiah, and on — each priestly family given its turn at the temple. Worship runs in orderly rotation. The eighth course, Abijah, would one day be Zacharias's, on duty when John's birth was announced.
The lots continue, assigning more of the twenty-four courses. Each takes its appointed week. God's house is served continually, no family idle and none overburdened, all in fair and steady order.

The lots continue, assigning more of the twenty-four courses. Each takes its appointed week. God's house is served continually, no family idle and none overburdened, all in fair and steady order.

The last courses are named, the whole ordered 'as the LORD God of Israel had commanded.' The arrangement follows divine appointment, not human preference. Acceptable worship keeps to the pattern God has given.

The rest of the Levites are listed, the assistants to the priestly courses. Every helper has his place. The work of God's house needs many hands, and each is recorded and honoured.

The rest of the Levites are listed, the assistants to the priestly courses. Every helper has his place. The work of God's house needs many hands, and each is recorded and honoured.
More Levite families are reckoned by their fathers' houses. The catalogue is careful and complete. God keeps account of all who serve Him, down to the least-known household.

More Levite families are reckoned by their fathers' houses. The catalogue is careful and complete. God keeps account of all who serve Him, down to the least-known household.

These Levites also cast lots, the older and younger families alike, before the king and the priests. None is favoured by age or rank. In God's service all stand equal before Him, each receiving his portion as from His hand.
